_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Grand Forks County, ND. Record survival shaped by the catastrophic 1997 flood and earlier record-keeping patterns.
display_name: Grand Forks County, North Dakota — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.location: Grand Forks — original frame and later brick courthouse
Grand Forks — brick courthouse on DeMers Avenue
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the current facility. The 1997 flood inundated parts of downtown Grand Forks but the courthouse records had been moved to the 1981 facility on higher ground.

record_losses.cause: Red River flood and downtown Grand Forks fire
records_destroyed: some city records stored in flooded basements
some justice of the peace records in ancillary locations
records_survived: probate files — all in the 1981 courthouse above flood level
record_losses.scope: minor for county records; significant for city records
record_losses.description: The 1997 Red River flood — the worst in Grand Forks history — inundated much of downtown and caused a fire that destroyed several blocks of the central business district. County records were protected in the 1981 courthouse, which was above the flood line. City records stored in flooded buildings suffered water damage. The 1997 event is a reminder of the chronic flood vulnerability that has shaped Grand Forks since its founding.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Grand Forks County's essential genealogical records survived the 1997 flood. Researchers should be aware, however, that city-level records (city directories, municipal court records, some school records) may have been damaged or lost in the flood.
record_losses.source: Parfit, Michael. 'The Floods of '97.' National Geographic 194, no. 3 (September 1998): 78-99.

deeds.gaps: No significant gaps. Federal homestead patents are recorded separately at the BLM General Land Office.
deeds.repository: Grand Forks County Recorder's Office; State Historical Society of North Dakota
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; BLM GLO Records for homestead patents

wills_estates.completeness: Good from 1880 forward. Few estates probated before that date due to the young, mobile population.
wills_estates.repository: Grand Forks County Clerk of Court; SHSND

marriages.completeness: Good. Norwegian Lutheran church marriages often preceded civil licensing.
marriages.gaps: Church marriages without civil license in the earliest settlement period.

court_minutes.gaps: Minor gaps in early territorial court records.
court_minutes.repository: Grand Forks County Clerk of Court; SHSND

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until approximately 1910, especially in rural townships. University of North Dakota records supplement for Grand Forks city.
vital_records.repository: ND Department of Health Vital Records; SHSND

substitute_sources.description: Grand Forks County had one of the densest concentrations of Norwegian Lutheran congregations in the United States. Ministerialbok records — baptisms, confirmations, marriages, burials — kept in Norwegian are essential genealogical sources.
substitute_sources.repository: NAHA, Northfield, MN; ELCA Archives; individual congregations; some at SHSND
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for Norwegian immigrant families. Often the only record of vital events before 1893.

substitute_sources.description: UND enrollment records, student directories, and yearbooks provide documentation of Grand Forks County residents attending the university from 1883 forward.
substitute_sources.repository: UND Chester Fritz Library, Special Collections, Grand Forks
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Identifies family members who attended college — enrollment records include hometown, parents' names (for early years), and sometimes photograph. Useful for tracking second-generation immigrant families.
substitute_sources.source: Geiger, University of the Northern Plains (1958).

substitute_sources.description: Same comprehensive homestead application files as described for Cass County.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for settlers. Application and proof-up files contain age, birthplace, family size, and witness testimony.

narrative_hooks: Grand Forks County's records survived the catastrophic 1997 flood because the courthouse had been moved to higher ground sixteen years earlier — but {ancestor_name}'s story in this county has always been shaped by water, from the spring floods that tested every generation to the disaster that remade the city.

If {ancestor_name} was baptized in a Norwegian Lutheran church in Grand Forks County, the minister's handwritten entry in the ministerialbok — recording the child's name, parents, date, and witnesses in Norwegian — may be the oldest surviving record of {pronoun_possessive} existence.

The absence of {ancestor_name} from Grand Forks County vital records before 1893 reflects the era, not the family — North Dakota did not require birth or death registration until that year, and rural compliance lagged for another decade.

Grand Forks County's homestead records at the National Archives contain {ancestor_name}'s own words — or at least the words {pronoun_subject} spoke to the land office clerk, describing the house {pronoun_subject} built, the acres {pronoun_subject} broke, and the neighbors who could testify that {pronoun_subject} had lived on the claim for five years.
source_references: State Historical Society of North Dakota. Guide to County Records. Bismarck: SHSND, 2003.
Parfit, Michael. 'The Floods of '97.' National Geographic 194, no. 3 (September 1998): 78-99.
Lovoll, Odd S. The Promise of America. Minneapolis: University of Minnesota Press, 1984.
Geiger, Louis G. University of the Northern Plains. Grand Forks: UND Press, 1958.
